title: Claim
description: A single falsifiable assertion extracted from a paper. The unit of structured knowledge in the rrxiv corpus. Claim graph edges (depends_on, supports, contradicts, extends) connect claims across papers.

id.description: Stable, globally unique claim ID. Once assigned, NEVER changes. Suggested format: <paper_id>:<local_label>.

paper_id.description: ID of the paper this claim originates in. Same paper may contain many claims.

statement.description: The claim itself, expressed as a single falsifiable assertion in natural language. Should be standalone-readable.

claim_type.description: What kind of claim this is.

evidence_type.description: What kind of evidence supports the claim within the paper.

scope.description: Conditions under which the claim is asserted to hold.

confidence.description: Author's stated confidence in the claim. Optional but encouraged.

depends_on.description: IDs of claims (in this or other papers) that this claim relies on. Forms the dependency edges of the claim graph.

supports.description: IDs of claims that this claim is presented as evidence for.

contradicts.description: IDs of claims this claim contradicts. Author asserts the contradiction; community may dispute.

extends.description: IDs of claims this claim extends or generalises.

replication_status.description: Aggregate replication status. **Server-derived** from annotations per RRP-0019: independent-replication count crosses the per-discipline quorum → `replicated`; contradicting annotations meet or exceed supports → `contradicted`; any non-superseded `claim_retraction` annotation (RRP-0020) → `retracted` (highest precedence). Author-provided values in submitted CIRs are ignored on response; clients should trust the server's derived value.

proof.description: Plain-text proof or evidence body that accompanies this claim. Extracted from the paired `evidence` environment in the source (parser pairs `\label{prop:X.Y}` to `\label{ev:X.Y}`). Multi-paragraph; preserves `$...$` math markers verbatim so renderers can hand off to KaTeX or MathJax. `\dependson` lines are stripped (the edge information already lives in `depends_on`). Optional; CIRs without a paired evidence block omit this field. See RRP-0015.

figures.description: Figures referenced inside the proof body via `\input{figures/...}`. Each entry records the source-relative path to the figure file and optionally its caption. Paths are relative to the paper's source-archive root (the tarball at `source.uri`). Optional; populated by the parser when the evidence block contains figure inputs. See RRP-0015.

source_location.description: Where in the source the claim originates, for provenance. Combines the prior section-level provenance fields with file/line span coordinates for the `\begin{claim}...\end{claim}` block plus the immediately-following `\begin{evidence}...\end{evidence}` block. Lines are 1-indexed. In multi-file papers, `file` is the source-archive-relative path resolved via the flatten-tex marker comments (`% [flatten-tex.py] inlined: <path>` / `% [flatten-tex.py] end: <path>`); single-file papers report the source filename. See RRP-0015.

file.description: Source-archive-relative path to the file containing the claim, e.g. `books/book01.tex`.

line_end.description: 1-indexed line number of the `\end{evidence}` (or `\end{claim}` when no evidence block follows) in `file`.

pdf_anchor.description: URL fragment that pins the rendered PDF to the page (or named destination) where the claim appears. Typically `#page=N` for raw page anchors, or `#nameddest=<id>` when the PDF carries named destinations. Optional; the parser leaves this null because computing the page number requires the rendered PDF cross-reference table (operators or downstream pipelines populate it). Specified here so future generators have a stable place to put it. See RRP-0015.

reproducibility.description: Author-attached reproducibility manifest for this claim. Optional; present when the paper's author publishes a reproduction recipe alongside the source. Distinct from replication annotations posted by external readers (those carry their own `reproducibility_manifest_uri`). The web client surfaces a 'Reproduce this' CTA when this field is populated. See RRP-0019 + reproducibility_manifest.schema.json.

manifest_uri.description: URL of a JSON document conforming to reproducibility_manifest.schema.json. Hosted externally (typically the paper repo's GitHub blob URL at a pinned tag).

manifest_hash.description: SHA-256 of the manifest's bytes, allowing clients to detect drift.

extracted_by.description: Who extracted this claim. Author-extracted claims are canonical from submission; others require confirmation.

canonical.description: True if confirmed by paper author or via quorum of credentialed annotators.

views_count.description: Server-stamped read counter (RRP-0022 / Sprint 22). Bumped on every GET /api/v0/claims/{id}. Only present on read responses — submitters MUST NOT populate this. Drives the `top_claims_by_views` leaderboard in /stats/pulse.

path.description: Source-archive-relative path to the figure file (e.g. `figures/fig-i-10.tex`). The figure is bundled in the paper's `source.tar.gz` and resolved relative to its root.

caption.description: Plain-text figure caption. Optional; the parser populates it when the figure file contains a `\caption{...}` macro.

examples.statement: A protocol that exposes claims as first-class structured entities is queryable by agents in ways an unstructured PDF corpus is not.
On a given finite straight line to construct an equilateral triangle.
examples.proof: Let $AB$ be the given finite straight line. With centre $A$ and distance $AB$ describe the circle $BCD$ (Postulate 3). With centre $B$ and distance $BA$ describe the circle $ACE$ (Postulate 3). From the point $C$, where the circles cut one another, draw $CA$ and $CB$ (Postulate 1). Since $A$ is the centre of $BCD$, $AC = AB$ (Definition I.15). Since $B$ is the centre of $ACE$, $BC = BA$ (Definition I.15). By Common Notion 1, $AC = BC$. Therefore the triangle $ABC$ is equilateral.

figures.caption: Proposition I.1. The two circles with centres $A$ and $B$ and common radius $AB$ meet at $C$; the triangle $ABC$ is equilateral.
